creator: empty.japan
caption:
I never shared this story before, but last year, right before moving to Nagano, I almost bought a traditional old house.

At the time, I couldn’t find any pet-friendly long-term rentals, and just when I was about to give up, I found it. It was absolutely gorgeous, in great condition, full of charm, and cost less than $50,000. I was already signing some of the initial papers when the seller notified me that the house was in a debris flow zone, which, after some research, became a hard no for me.

But I couldn’t let go of that idea. Owning an old traditional Japanese house was something I’d always wanted. So I kept looking. And the more I searched, the more I learned.

Turns out, the age of these houses matters even more than I thought. Older ones often don’t meet modern earthquake standards, since Japan updates its building regulations regularly. And if you’re aiming for a place that feels truly livable—safe, insulated, and not freezing in winter—renovating an akiya can actually cost more than building something new from scratch. Especially if major repairs, core structure and all, are involved.

I also learned that some old houses were built using materials like asbestos. Honestly, I had no idea.

Akiyas are cheap for a reason, and it’s easy to get tempted without fully considering the downsides. It’s kind of like buying a vintage car—it has so much charm, but finding one in great condition is hard, and restoration costs can add up quickly depending on the work needed.

By no means am I saying that buying an akiya is a bad idea, it can truly be an amazing option. I just couldn’t find the house that met all my needs.

So instead… I decided to build one.
